subtopics.title: Political Parties - Definition and Functions
common_misconceptions: Political parties and pressure groups are the same
One-party system means only one party is allowed (could be dominant party system)
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: First Republic (1960-66): AG, NPC, NCNC, NNDP. Second Republic (1979-83): NPN, UPN, NPP, PRP, GNPP, NAP. Fourth Republic (1999-present): PDP, APC, APGA, Labour Party, NNPP. Multi-party system. INEC registers parties. Functions: aggregate interests, select candidates (primaries), form government, opposition, political education. Party systems: one-party (China), two-party (USA - Democrats/Republicans), multi-party (most European countries, Nigeria). Criticisms of Nigerian parties: lack of ideology, ethnic/regional bases, godfatherism, election rigging, party switching.

subtopics.title: Pressure Groups - Types and Functions
common_misconceptions: Pressure groups are anti-government (they seek to influence, not overthrow)
All pressure groups use violent methods
Pressure groups are illegal in Nigeria
subtopics.teaching_note: Pressure groups: organized groups seeking to influence government policy without seeking power. Types: Promotional (promote causes - human rights, environment), Protective (protect member interests - trade unions, professional bodies). Nigerian examples: NLC (Nigeria Labour Congress), NUT (Nigeria Union of Teachers), NBA (Nigerian Bar Association), NMA (Nigerian Medical Association), civil society groups (SERAP, Enough is Enough, BBOG). Methods: lobbying, protests, strikes, media campaigns, litigation, dialogue. Difference from political parties: don't contest elections, focus on specific issues, can be non-partisan.

subtopics.title: Local Government in Nigeria
common_misconceptions: Local governments are created by the federal government (created by state governments)
LG chairmen are appointed (elected under 1999 Constitution)
Local governments have no constitutional backing
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: 1976 reform by Obasanjo (military) created uniform LG system. 1999 Constitution: Section 7 guarantees democratically elected LG councils. Functions: primary education, healthcare, roads, markets, sanitation, birth/death registration, agriculture extension. Sources: federal allocations (Federation Account), state allocations, rates/tenement, licenses, fines. 774 LGs in Nigeria. Problems: financial dependence on state/federal, state government interference, corruption, poor funding, creation of LCDAs (development areas) by some states without constitutional backing. LG autonomy debate: ongoing calls for direct funding from federal, removal of state joint accounts.
